title: Quantum resource localizability transitions in deep thermalization
authors: Xiaozhou Feng, Chang Liu, Zihan Cheng, Wen Wei Ho, Matteo Ippoliti
abstract: We investigate how quantum resource constraints affect deep thermalization, the emergence of universal local wavefunction distributions from partial measurements of a quantum many-body state. Quantum resources, such as non-stabilizerness (magic), coherence, asymmetry, imaginarity, and non-Gaussianity, are essential for quantum information processing, and constraints on their global abundance can reshape these emergent distributions. To address this question, we develop a unified framework for deep thermalization within general quantum resource theories (QRTs). Our central result is that QRTs fall into two classes: ``smoothly localizable'' (SL) QRTs, where the resource content of local post-measurement states changes continuously with the global resource density, set by the initial state and measurement basis, yielding continuously tunable wavefunction distributions; and ``threshold localizable'' (TL) QRTs, where the local resource content jumps discontinuously from minimal to near-maximal past a critical global resource threshold, producing a sharp transition between a resourceless, ``deep-ergodicity breaking'' distribution and a resourceful, maximally random one. We trace this SL-TL dichotomy to an information-theoretic mechanism, block sharpening: by viewing each QRT as coherence between blocks in Hilbert space, we show that the local resource content depends on the measurement's ability to collapse an initial superposition into a single resourceless block. Our theory is analytically tractable and quantitatively predicts the phase boundaries across all studied QRTs, which we validate with extensive numerical simulations. Finally, we highlight two consequences: a novel magic transition in zero-rate quantum error-correcting codes--previously believed to occur only at finite rates--and new implications for quantum resource certification protocols based on post-measurement state ensembles.
summary: The paper develops a unified theory classifying how global constraints on quantum resources affect deep thermalization. It shows that these constraints force the system into one of two regimes: smooth resource changes (SL) or sharp phase transitions (TL). This framework predicts novel critical points, including magic transitions in error-correcting codes.
main_problem: The paper investigates how constraints imposed by various quantum resources affect deep thermalization, which is the emergence of universal local wavefunction distributions from partial measurements.
main_result: Quantum Resource Theories (QRTs) governing this process fall into two classes: 'smoothly localizable' (SL) leading to continuous resource changes, and 'threshold localizable' (TL) exhibiting sharp phase transitions at critical global resource thresholds.
method: The authors develop a unified framework using an information-theoretic mechanism called 'block sharpening' to analyze the dependence of local post-measurement state resources on global initial resources.
model_or_system: The study analyzes general quantum many-body systems undergoing dynamics constrained by specific quantum resources like non-stabilizerness, coherence, and asymmetry. The analysis is performed in the thermodynamic limit using projected ensembles (PE) from partial measurements.
key_observables: Resource content of the PE ($R_{PE}$), average post-measurement purity ($	ext{Tr}(ho_{m,A})$), and resource measures like magic or charge variance ($R_Q$).
important_parameters: Global resource density ($R_{in}$), critical global resource threshold ($R_{in,crit}$), and system size ratios ($N_A/N$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on mapping QRTs to subspace coherence and utilizes approximations like the random unitary assumption (Haar measure) and concentration inequalities.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the SL vs. TL dichotomy in resource content variation, show block sharpening mechanisms via set intersections ($I \cap F$), and map out phase boundaries for various resources.
paper_structure: The paper establishes the problem of resource localizability in deep thermalization, develops the unified QRT framework based on block sharpening, classifies QRTs into SL/TL types, derives quantitative predictions for phase boundaries using entropy methods (mixed/tilted basis), and concludes by applying these findings to specific physical examples like QEC codes.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful theoretical tool—the resource localizability criterion—to distinguish between different types of quantum dynamics in highly interacting, non-equilibrium many-body systems, linking fundamental information theory concepts directly to thermalization physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: (a) Setting of our work. We study deep thermalization through the lens of quantum resource theories (QRTs). We consider systems where a quantum resource (depicted by lightning symbols) is injected through the initial state and the final measurements, and is scrambled by free (i.e., resource-preserving) unitary dynamics. Our work identifies two fundamentally distinct classes of QRTs: (b) “smoothly-localizable” (SL) QRTs, where the resource content RPE of the PE varies smoothly with the globally injected resource Rin, and (c) “threshold-localizable” (TL) QRTs, where the resource content of the PE RPE undergoes a sharp transition from being zero to being near maximal, past a certain...

FIG. 2: Schematic illustration of the block sharpening mechanism. The Hilbert space H is depicted as a collection of bitstrings (circles) arranged into blocks Bi defined by a QRT [Eq. (5)] (black contours). The ket |Ψ0⟩and bra ⟨Ψm| in Eq. (8) contain a set of bitstrings (‘+’ and ‘×’ symbols respectively) that populate two subsets of the blocks, I (shaded red) and F (shaded blue). The free dynamics fully scrambles each bitstring within its block. The overlap [Eq. (8)] receives contributions only from blocks that belong to both I and F (hatched). (a) Resourceless phase. Below the resource threshold, the subsets I and F are so sparse that they typically do not intersect. (b) Critical point. At...

FIG. 3: Localizability transitions in the QRTs of syn- drome coherence and mixedness / classical randomness. (a) Syndrome coherence. Average resource of PE states Rs as a function of parameters α0,m, within one in- stance of a random parity-check code of rate 1 −σ. We choose NA = 2, α0 = 1/4 and σ = 3/4, while αm is varied; every data point is averaged over 1000 real- izations; each realization uses a random independently sampled parity check code. The vertical dashed line is the predicted critical point αm + α0 = σ, the horizon- tal dotted line is the value for the Haar ensemble. (b) Mixedness/classical randomness. Average PE resource RP = −EEPE log2 P

FIG. 4: Smooth localizability of imaginarity and U(1)- asymmetry. All results are for NA = 2. (a) Average imaginarity of the PE, RI, as a function of the imagi- narity RI,in of the initial state under Haar-random real orthogonal dynamics (see Sec. V A). The theoretical pre- diction Eq. (46) at small α is depicted by the dashed line (here the slope is 3/4), showing good agreement. The average imaginarity of a Haar random ensemble is indi- cated by the horizontal dashed line. (b) Averaged charge variance RQ of the PE versus charge-variance density RQ,in/N of the input state under random asymmetry- free dynamics (see Sec. V B). The horizontal dotted line is the corresponding Haar-random value.

FIG. 5: (a) Physical realization of our operator evolution setup in 1D as qubits arranged in a ladder geometry, depicted for the case of magic. An initial operator |u(θ, n)⟩⟩⊗N (each u(θ, n) corresponds to a red circle) is evolved under a random Clifford unitary channel UCl ⊗U ∗ Cl, then the bath B is measured in the “phase basis”, Eq. (54).
